name: Mean-value theorem (Lagrange) for derivatives
principles: continuity on closed interval + differentiability on open interior
existence of an intermediate point matching the average rate of change
Rolle's theorem as a special case (zero secant slope)
formulaTeX: f \text{ continuous on } [a, b], \; \text{differentiable on } (a, b) \;\Longrightarrow\; \exists\, c \in (a, b): \; f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}
conventions: f is a real-valued function of a single real variable
[a, b] is a closed bounded interval with a < b
the conclusion is existence — c is not unique in general (multiple c may satisfy the condition)
real-analysis result: complex-analysis analogue requires holomorphicity and gives different conclusions
vector-valued generalisation (Cauchy mean-value theorem) replaces both sides with parametric ratio
discrete analogue (finite-difference mean value): f(b) - f(a) = (b - a) f'(xi) holds at a discrete grid point only approximately, with error controlled by f''
expectedLimits: f constant on [a, b]: f(b) - f(a) = 0, so f'(c) = 0 for any c in (a, b) — trivially satisfied
Rolle's theorem (special case f(a) = f(b)): exists c with f'(c) = 0
f(x) = mx + b (linear): f'(c) = m for all c — every point witnesses the theorem
endpoints excluded: differentiability at the open interior is required; f'(a) or f'(b) need not exist
fails for non-differentiable kinks: f(x) = |x| on [-1, 1] has slope 0 between endpoints, but f'(c) jumps at 0 — no interior c with f'(c) = 0
implies: if f' = 0 throughout (a, b), then f is constant; foundation of the fundamental theorem of calculus
references: Cauchy (1823) — Résumé des leçons données à l'École royale polytechnique
Rudin — Principles of Mathematical Analysis, ch.5
